names.scientific_name: Fatty acids (various saturated and unsaturated carboxylic acids)

natural_sources: All fats and oils (animal and plant)
Coconut oil, palm oil (saturated fatty acids)
Olive oil, canola oil (unsaturated fatty acids)
natural_occurrence.description: Fatty acids are natural components of all fats and oils. They are released when fats are hydrolyzed (split apart). E570 includes various saturated and unsaturated fatty acids typically derived from vegetable oils or animal fats.

manufacturing.description: Produced by hydrolysis (splitting) of fats and oils using water, enzymes, or alkaline treatment. The resulting fatty acids are separated, purified, and may be further processed (hydrogenation, fractionation). Common sources include palm oil, coconut oil, and tallow.

other_uses.cosmetics: Emulsifier, emollient, thickener in creams, lotions, soaps.
other_uses.industrial: Soap making (saponification), lubricants, candle making, plastics, rubber.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Suppository bases, tablet lubricants, topical formulations.

eu.conditions: Permitted in various food categories as glazing agent, anti-foaming agent, and release agent.

usa.cfr_reference: 21 CFR 172.860 (various sections for different fatty acids)
usa.notes: Widely used as GRAS substances, naturally present in foods.

fun_facts: Not a single chemical - E570 is a category covering many different fatty acids naturally found in food
The same molecules that make up the fats you eat every day - butter is about 10% free fatty acids
Used to make SOAP through saponification (reaction with lye) - been doing this for thousands of years
Stearic acid (common E570 component) is also used to make candles
Completely natural and already part of your diet from fats and oils
Different fatty acids have different properties: saturated ones are solid at room temp, unsaturated are liquid
